_readme: RailKing Gen6 course manifest - first-pass draft generated from CBT_Outline_RailKing_G6.md.
All content screen HTML files referenced here are planned authoring targets in the workspace root.
Update narration, questions, and LMS metadata before final build and release.

course.title: RailKing Gen6 Operations and Service Fundamentals

kla.description: This course prepares RailKing Gen6 operators and service personnel to locate and apply the correct operating, reference, and support documentation; execute safe startup, operation, and shutdown workflows; and use RK24 drawings and OEM vendor references for advanced troubleshooting.
kla.revision_notes: Initial manifest draft from approved outline.

intro.subtitle: Operator and Service Reference Workflow Training

screens.label: Title Card
vo: Welcome to RailKing Gen6 Operations and Service Fundamentals. This course shows you how to use the RailKing document set to run the unit safely, respond to issues, and hand off clean technical information for service support.

screens.label: Who This Course Is For
vo: This training is designed for operators, lead operators, field supervisors, and service technicians who support RailKing Gen6 equipment in daily operation and troubleshooting.

screens.label: Course Application
vo: You will practice selecting the right manual, following startup and shutdown workflows, and using model-specific references including RK24 drawings and OEM vendor documentation to solve field problems.

screens.label: Learning Objectives
vo: By the end of this course, you will be able to complete six core tasks.
One. Identify the RailKing Gen6 document set and where to find operating, service, and support information.
Two. Explain model family differences and common operating principles across RK22, RK23, and RK24.
Three. Perform safe pre-operation checks and startup sequencing using the operator guidance.
Four. Operate the machine through normal work cycles and monitor key indicators and limits.
Five. Execute shutdown, post-operation checks, and escalation and support workflows.
Six. Apply model-specific service references, drawings, and OEM documentation to troubleshoot and maintain RK24 systems.
These objectives establish the baseline workflow for confident RailKing Gen6 operation and service coordination.

objectives.text: Identify the RailKing Gen6 document set and where to find operating, service, and support information.
Explain model family differences and common operating principles across RK22, RK23, and RK24.
Perform safe pre-operation checks and startup sequencing using the operator guidance.
Operate the machine through normal work cycles and monitor key indicators and limits.
Execute shutdown, post-operation checks, and escalation and support workflows.
Apply model-specific service references, drawings, and OEM documentation to troubleshoot and maintain RK24 systems.

int2_cards.body: This course supports operators and lead operators who need repeatable startup, operation, and shutdown workflows.
It also supports field supervisors and support coordinators who prepare complete handoff packets for service escalation.
The final section extends to service technicians using RK24 drawings and OEM vendor references for advanced troubleshooting.

int3_kp.body:
Primary workflow:
Find the right source first, then execute the procedure.

Reference stack:
Operator guide, model manuals, support forms, RK24 drawings, and OEM vendor documents.

intro.int3_highlight: ⚡ Correct documentation selection prevents rework, delays, and unsafe field actions.

int3_closing.body: Consistent workflows and complete service handoffs improve uptime and decision quality across operations and maintenance teams.

screens.label: Section Intro
screens.vo: Welcome to Section 1. This section introduces the three-tier document ecosystem for RailKing Gen6 equipment, your role boundaries as an operator, and the support channels you will use when escalation is required. By the end of this section you will know exactly which document to reach for and when to stop and call for help.

screens.label: Document Library Map
screens.vo: The RailKing Gen6 documentation is organised in three tiers. The G6 Operator Reference Guide covers shared workflow principles for all models. Model-specific manuals — RK22, RK23, and RK24 — cover specifications, thresholds, and procedures unique to each unit. For the RK24, the C5 drawing set and C6 OEM vendor package complete the advanced service reference stack.

screens.label: Safety and Responsibility
screens.vo: Operator responsibilities are defined before any machine interaction. Your authority is bounded — execute approved procedures, monitor indicators, and escalate when conditions move outside your role. The non-negotiable safety rules on this screen apply every shift, without exception regardless of schedule pressure or familiarity with the equipment.

screens.label: Warranty and Limits
screens.vo: Warranty coverage depends on correct documentation and staying within approved procedures. Review the four warranty outcome categories — covered, review required, voided, and reference only. When an action is outside your authority, the warranty document defines what is at stake. Knowing the boundaries before you act is always easier than explaining an exception afterwards.

screens.label: Contacts and Support Path
screens.vo: When a fault or defect exceeds your authority to resolve, the support path has five escalation stages. Know your site supervisor, the RailKing dealer contact, and when to move from one stage to the next. Submitting a complete, well-documented request on the first attempt is the single biggest factor in reducing response time and getting the machine back to service quickly.

screens.label: Forms and Data Quality
screens.vo: There are four standard forms — parts orders, equipment technical support, technical manual feedback, and sales feedback. Each form has a specific purpose and a defined set of required fields. A form with complete, accurate data takes the same time to fill in as an incomplete one — but produces a far better outcome. The good and poor entry examples on the right side of this screen show exactly what that difference looks like in practice.

quiz.quiz_vo: Complete this section checkpoint to confirm you can select the right source and follow the support workflow.

pool.stem: Which document should be used first for day-to-day operating workflow guidance?

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Operator guidance is the first reference for normal workflow.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Start with operator guidance before specialized references.

pool.stem: What is the main purpose of standardized support forms?

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Complete data improves support response quality.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. The forms are for structured, complete support information.

pool.stem: When should escalation occur during uncertainty about safe limits?

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Escalate immediately when safe operating limits are uncertain.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Do not continue when limits are unclear.

pool.stem: Which source is most appropriate for support contact pathways?

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. contacts.pdf defines primary support channels.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Contact pathways are maintained in contacts.pdf.

pool.stem: Which form should be used when ordering replacement parts?

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Parts orders use form_parts_order.pdf.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. There is a dedicated parts order form for this purpose.

pool.stem: A technician finds a discrepancy in a manual. Which form is most appropriate?

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Technical manual feedback has a dedicated form.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Use form_tech_manual_feedback.pdf for documentation discrepancies.

pool.stem: Which document outlines coverage limits for non-routine actions?

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. warranty.pdf defines coverage and responsibility boundaries.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Warranty context and limits are documented in warranty.pdf.

screens.label: Section Intro
screens.vo: Section 2 maps the three Gen6 models — RK22, RK23, and RK24 — to their shared architecture and their model-specific differences. Understanding where the models converge and where they diverge determines which document you reach for and how quickly you can make a safe, confident decision in the field.

screens.label: Model Family Comparison
screens.vo: The RK22, RK23, and RK24 share a common operational philosophy but differ in rated capacity, configuration, and available technology. The RK24 is the most advanced unit in the family — it includes the IFM electronic control system, the C5 engineering drawing set, and the C6 OEM vendor documentation package. Always verify the model from the data plate before applying any specification or threshold value.

screens.label: Common Subsystems
screens.vo: The Gen6 family shares nine core subsystems across all models — shown in blue on this diagram. The orange blocks represent RK24-specific extensions — subsystems that exist only on that model and require dedicated OEM documentation for any service work. Use the G6 Reference Guide for decisions about the shared systems. Switch to model-specific references the moment an orange-block subsystem is involved.

screens.label: Operator Interface Overview
screens.vo: The operator display is the primary window into machine status. On the RK22 and RK23 it uses a conventional gauge and warning lamp layout. The RK24 adds the IFM CR1081 digital display with active fault code reporting and a structured menu system. Before responding to any indicator, confirm which model you are operating — the procedure that follows may differ between models in ways that matter.

screens.label: When to Use Model Manuals
screens.vo: Use the G6 Reference Guide for workflow and process decisions — startup gates, escalation paths, and handoff structure. Switch to the model-specific manual as soon as any number is involved — thresholds, fault codes, fluid types, torque values, or service intervals. The three-column decision guide on this screen gives you the trigger condition, the response, and the correct manual to open. If you are not sure which manual applies, that uncertainty is itself a reason to stop and check before proceeding.

quiz.quiz_vo: Answer these questions to confirm your model-family reference strategy.

pool.stem: What is the safest approach when guidance appears to differ across models?

options.text: Use RK24 guidance for all models

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Model-specific manuals resolve model-specific differences.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Always validate model-specific instructions.

pool.stem: Which set best represents model-level source documents?

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Those three manuals provide model-level guidance.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Model-level documents are the RK22/RK23/RK24 manuals.

pool.stem: Why distinguish common subsystems from model-specific details?

options.text: To speed up safe decision making

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. It improves speed while preserving correctness.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. The goal is faster, safer decisions.

pool.stem: What should you do before applying a procedure to a different model?

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Validate procedure applicability by model.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Cross-model assumptions are unsafe.

pool.stem: Which model in the Gen6 family includes the most advanced diagnostics capability?

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. The RK24 includes advanced diagnostics and OEM vendor integration.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. The RK24 is the advanced configuration in the family.

pool.stem: Which document should a supervisor consult for shared operating principles?

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. The Operator Reference Guide covers shared Gen6 principles.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. The G6 Operator Reference Guide is the cross-model resource.

pool.stem: An operator needs threshold values for the RK23. What is the correct action?

options.text: Use RK24 values as they are more advanced
Consult the RK23 model manual for model-specific thresholds

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Always use the model-specific manual for thresholds.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. RK23 thresholds must come from the RK23 manual.

screens.label: Section Intro
screens.vo: Section 3 follows the operator through pre-operation, startup sequencing, and the hold-point framework that prevents unsafe progression. Three go and no-go gates structure the entire startup process — and none of them may be bypassed for any reason.

screens.label: Pre-Op Inspection Zones
screens.vo: The walk-around covers four zones in a defined sequence — front and right side, rear, left side, and undercarriage. Each zone has a specific inspection focus described in the zone diagram on the left. Any finding that prevents safe operation is a hold condition — it stops your progress at Gate 1 before you ever touch the control panel.

screens.label: Control Panel Readiness
screens.vo: The control panel has four monitoring zones — engine and power, hydraulic system, air and brake circuit, and transmission and drive. Before engaging the starter, four conditions must be confirmed: master power on, neutral selected, air reservoirs building, and no active fault lamps. The five-step pre-ignition sequence on the right side of this screen completes Gate 2 of the startup process.

screens.label: Startup Sequence
screens.vo: The startup sequence has six steps and three go and no-go gates. Gate 1 is a clean walk-around. Gate 2 is panel readiness with no active fault lamps. Gate 3 is warm-up confirmation — all four key systems in their operating band before the first load is applied. In cold conditions extend the warm-up period. The hydraulic pump is the most vulnerable component at low operating temperature, and rushing it causes premature wear.

screens.label: Abnormal Hold Points
screens.vo: Five hold conditions can interrupt the startup sequence. An amber lamp is a hold — identify the fault code and determine if continuation is safe before proceeding. A red lamp is a no-start condition, full stop. Oil pressure failure after crank is an immediate shutdown. Abnormal noise and air pressure failure round out the five conditions. The operator boundary is clear: amber means identify and assess, red means stop and escalate.

screens.label: Startup Confirmation and Records
screens.vo: Before releasing the park brake, six confirmation items must all be checked green. The shift log entry is not optional — even a clean start with no faults requires a record. A complete entry includes unit ID, date, operator name, hour meter reading, fault codes observed or the word none, and any anomalies noted during the walk-around. Missing entries create gaps that become problems when a fault pattern develops over time.

quiz.quiz_vo: Complete the startup workflow check before moving to normal operations.
quiz.fail_msg: Review Section 3 startup process and retry.

pool.stem: What is the first major phase before startup command execution?

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Pre-operation inspection is required before startup actions.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Begin with pre-operation inspection and readiness checks.

pool.stem: When should a startup hold point be used?

options.text: When conditions are unverified or abnormal

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Hold points are for unverified or abnormal conditions.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Hold when criteria are not satisfied.

pool.stem: Why is startup recordkeeping required?

options.text: To support shift continuity and traceability

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Records preserve continuity and escalation context.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Records are essential for continuity and troubleshooting.

pool.stem: If a readiness prerequisite is missing, the operator should:

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Stop, resolve, or escalate as defined.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Never proceed with missing prerequisites.

pool.stem: Which zone should be inspected first in the pre-operation sequence?

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Perimeter and safety is the first inspection zone.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Start with perimeter and safety before other zones.

pool.stem: How long should a unit run at idle before applying operating load?

options.text: As specified in the model manual
At least 30 seconds regardless of conditions

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Follow model-manual idle warm-up specifications.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Warm-up time is model-specific and procedure-defined.

pool.stem: What should be confirmed before moving from startup to operational mode?

options.text: All hold point criteria are satisfied

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. All hold point criteria must be satisfied before progression.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Hold points gate progression — satisfy all criteria first.

screens.label: Section Intro
screens.vo: Section 4 covers the normal operating workflow — monitoring key system indicators, applying operator versus service-level adjustments, and responding to warnings in a structured way. The discipline you apply during normal operation is the single biggest factor in how often the machine reaches an unexpected shutdown.

screens.label: Operating Cycle Overview
screens.vo: The normal operating cycle has four phases — startup and warm-up, productive work, cool-down, and shutdown. Monitoring occurs continuously throughout, but the structured checkpoints shown here are your defined opportunities to confirm all four key systems are within their bands and trending correctly. A trend moving toward the caution threshold is worth noting even before any alarm activates.

screens.label: Indicators and Thresholds
screens.vo: Four systems require active monitoring during every shift — engine coolant temperature, engine oil pressure, hydraulic oil temperature, and air reservoir pressure. Each has an operating band, an amber caution threshold, and a red shutdown boundary. The values shown here are representative Gen6 ranges. Always verify the exact limits for your specific model in the model manual — do not assume the numbers are identical across models.

screens.label: Common Adjustments
screens.vo: Operators control inputs — throttle, direction, speed range, and hydraulic flow rate. Technicians adjust components — relief valve pressure, flow control valves, governor settings, and brake proportioning. The boundary table on the right side of this screen is your reference when any adjustment decision comes up. If the adjustment involves opening a component or changing a calibrated setting, it belongs on the service side of the line.

screens.label: Responding to Warnings
screens.vo: Warning response follows a five-step hierarchy. When a gauge approaches its band limit, reduce load proactively before any alarm activates. An amber lamp triggers a safe stop and fault code lookup. A red lamp requires an immediate stop — lower any raised load, set the park brake, then shut down. Once the machine is secured, complete the fault report and escalate. Resetting a warning lamp without identifying the cause is never an acceptable response.

screens.label: Operator Handoff
screens.vo: A complete operator handoff takes five steps. The outgoing operator brings the machine to safe hold, completes the shift log, and delivers a verbal brief. The incoming operator performs their own walk-around before signing. Both operators sign the log — the outgoing operator to close the shift, the incoming operator to formally accept responsibility. An incoming operator who skips the walk-around has accepted a machine they have not inspected.

quiz.quiz_vo: Check your understanding of normal operation and warning response.
quiz.fail_msg: Review Section 4 and retake the quiz.

pool.stem: How should indicator thresholds be applied?

options.text: Use one fixed threshold set for all models

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Confirm thresholds against the correct model references.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Threshold interpretation must be model-validated.

pool.stem: What is the best response to an unexpected warning?

options.text: Apply warning response procedure and assess escalation

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Follow procedure and escalate based on criteria.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Warnings require procedural response, not suppression.

pool.stem: Why is operator handoff quality important?

options.text: It preserves operating context and pending actions

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Strong handoff avoids missed context and repeated work.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Handoff is critical for continuity and safety.

pool.stem: Which practice supports stable normal operation?

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Procedure-driven monitoring stabilizes operations.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Stability comes from disciplined, procedural operation.

pool.stem: At which operating cycle phase should interval logs be captured?

options.text: During stable operational load per schedule
Logs are not required during normal operation

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Interval checks should be logged during the stable load phase.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Logging happens during operation on a defined schedule.

pool.stem: A yellow indicator light activates during normal operation. What is the immediate correct action?

options.text: Apply warning response procedure and assess escalation
Reset the panel and hope it clears

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Follow warning response procedure before making an escalation decision.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Every warning requires a procedure-driven response.

pool.stem: What should a handoff communication include?

options.text: Active observations, pending actions, and current status
Parts ordered in the last 30 days

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Handoff must include full operating context.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. A complete handoff includes status, observations, and pending items.

sections.title: Shutdown, Recovery, and Daily Service Interface

screens.label: Section Intro
screens.vo: Section 5 covers the end of the operational cycle — controlled shutdown, post-operation inspection, and the service handoff packet. The quality of your work at the end of a shift directly determines the quality of the next shift's start and the speed of any service response that follows.

screens.label: Normal Shutdown Sequence
screens.vo: The normal shutdown sequence mirrors the startup sequence in reverse — finish the work cycle, reduce load, reach a safe position, reduce to low idle, and execute shutdown after the cool-down period. Never shut down from high load. The turbocharger requires oil flow to cool its bearings after the engine stops, and skipping the cool-down period shortens turbo life significantly.

screens.label: Securing the Unit
screens.vo: A machine is not secured until six conditions are confirmed — attachment lowered to ground, park brake engaged, transmission in neutral or park, engine off, key removed, and master electrical isolated for extended parking. Leaving a machine with a raised load or key in the ignition is a site safety exposure regardless of how long you expect to be away from the cab.

screens.label: Post-Operation Inspection
screens.vo: The post-operation inspection runs the same six zones as the walk-around — structural, fluids, fluid levels, tyres and tracks, attachments, and the log entry. Defects are classified red, amber, or green. A red defect puts the machine out of service before the next shift starts. Note any fluid quantity added during the inspection — unusual consumption is an early fault signal that is easy to miss if you're not tracking it.

screens.label: Service Handoff Packet
screens.vo: When a machine goes to service, the operator assembles the handoff packet before handing over the key. The packet includes the complete shift log, a completed equipment tech support form, the current hour meter reading, and photos of any visible defects. Incomplete packets delay the repair — a technician who arrives without complete context must reconstruct information you already had when you drove it in.

quiz.quiz_vo: Complete this checkpoint on shutdown and service handoff quality.

pool.stem: What is the primary objective of a controlled shutdown?

options.text: Reach and verify a safe stable state

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Controlled shutdown ensures safe and verified state transition.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Safety and stable post-operation state are the primary goals.

pool.stem: Why is a post-operation inspection required?

options.text: To confirm condition and detect issues early

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Post-operation checks identify conditions requiring action.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Inspection supports early detection and better handoff quality.

pool.stem: What improves service response time most?

options.text: Complete handoff packet with required forms and observations

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Complete handoff packets reduce back-and-forth and delays.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Service teams need full context and structured data.

pool.stem: If shutdown criteria are not met, you should:

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Unmet criteria require documented exception handling.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Do not finalize without resolving or escalating exceptions.

pool.stem: How long should a unit idle before executing shutdown?

options.text: No idle required — shut down from load directly

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. A 2–3 minute idle stabilization period is required before shutdown.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. A minimum idle period is required before executing shutdown.

pool.stem: What is the purpose of securing the unit after shutdown?

options.text: To prevent accidental restart and ensure known safe state

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Securing prevents accidental restart and confirms safe state.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Securing is a safety requirement after every shutdown.

pool.stem: Which element is NOT typically part of a post-operation inspection?

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Parts ordering is a follow-up action, not part of the inspection itself.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Inspection observes and records condition — ordering is a separate step.

sections.title: Advanced Service Reference (RK24 Drawings and OEM Docs)

screens.label: Section Intro
screens.vo: Section 6 extends to service technicians working with the RK24 advanced reference package. This section covers the C5 engineering drawing set, the 31-document C6 OEM vendor package, and a case study that traces a hydraulic fault from observed symptom through the complete document chain to the repair action.

screens.label: RK24 Drawing Navigation
screens.vo: The C5 drawing set contains 58 engineering drawings organised by assembly and system. Navigate by identifying the symptom domain first — hydraulic circuit, structural, electrical, or powertrain — then locate the relevant drawing family. The drawing number prefix identifies the system domain and is the fastest path to the correct sheet without scanning the entire index.

screens.label: OEM Bundles by Subsystem
screens.vo: Thirty-one OEM documents across sixteen vendor families are grouped into ten subsystem bundles in this interactive table. Click any row to expand the documents in that bundle. The rule is simple — identify the subsystem first, then select from the correct bundle. Applying a document from one subsystem to diagnose a different one is a common error that wastes time and produces incorrect conclusions.

screens.label: Hydraulic and Pneumatic References
screens.vo: Five vendor families cover hydraulic and pneumatic systems on the RK24 — Rexroth for pumps and directional valves, Marzocchi for the auxiliary gear pump, Sun Hydraulics for cartridge valves, Hydac for filtration, and Bendix with AAA Valve for the air and brake circuits. Each diagnostic path on the right panel identifies the fault type and directs you to the specific document that answers it.

screens.label: Controls and Camera References
screens.vo: The IFM system on the RK24 comprises nine documents covering the CR0020 mobile controller, the CR1081 display, and the camera subsystem. The CR0020 is the fault source — the CR1081 is the display window. A fault code appearing on the display originates in the controller software logic. When diagnosing a display fault, open the CR0020 Software Manual first, then cross-reference the CR1081 System Manual for the display-side behaviour.

screens.label: Powertrain and Auxiliary References
screens.vo: Four vendor families cover the RK24 powertrain — John Deere for the engine, Funk for the two gearbox variants, CTD for the hub-wheel assembly, and a family of auxiliary system vendors for cab and ancillary components. Engine warranty work must be performed by an authorised John Deere dealer. For the Funk gearboxes, verify the variant from the data plate — CTM147 and CTM157 are not interchangeable and require different service procedures.

screens.label: Symptom-to-Action Case Study
screens.vo: In this case study, one symptom — sluggish hydraulics with elevated temperature — leads through four separate OEM documents before reaching a root cause. The operator observed and recorded. The technician decoded the fault code, mapped the circuit, verified the pump specification, and closed the repair with a service record. Without the operator log entry, the technician starts at square one. This is why every shift entry matters.

quiz.quiz_vo: Complete this final section checkpoint on RK24 advanced reference use.
quiz.pass_msg: Excellent. Continue to the course summary.
quiz.fail_msg: Review Section 6 advanced reference workflow and retry.

pool.stem: What is the first step in advanced RK24 troubleshooting?

options.text: Select correct drawing and document domain

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Correct source selection is the first step.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Start by identifying the correct technical reference domain.

pool.stem: Why cross-reference service manual and OEM documentation?

options.text: To align system-level and component-level guidance

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Cross-referencing aligns system context with component detail.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Both document levels are needed for accurate diagnosis.

pool.stem: Which statement best describes the RK24 C5 and C6 package roles?

options.text: C5 is drawings; C6 is OEM vendor documentation
C5 is warranty; C6 is contacts

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. C5 and C6 together support advanced troubleshooting workflows.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. C5 is drawing-centric and C6 is OEM-document-centric.

pool.stem: What does a high-quality advanced service handoff include?

options.text: Symptom, references consulted, findings, and pending actions

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Complete context accelerates advanced support diagnosis.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Service teams need full reference and finding context.

pool.stem: What are the two main document packages in the RK24 advanced reference set?

options.text: C5 Drawings and C6 OEM Vendor documentation

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. C5 contains drawings; C6 contains OEM vendor documentation.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. The advanced packages are C5 (drawings) and C6 (OEM vendors).

pool.stem: The IFM CR0020 mobile controller documentation would be found in which package?

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. OEM vendor component manuals are in the C6 package.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Component-level OEM docs belong to the C6 vendor package.

pool.stem: For a hydraulic pressure fault, which source should you consult first?

options.text: Hydraulic schematics in the C5 drawing set

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Hydraulic schematics in C5 are the primary reference for pressure faults.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Start with the hydraulic schematics in the C5 drawing set.

screens.label: Key Takeaways
screens.vo: Six sections, six takeaways. From Section 1 — know your documents before you need them. From Section 2 — the three models are not the same machine. From Section 3 — three startup gates, all must pass. From Section 4 — monitor trend, not just snapshot. From Section 5 — a shift closes with a complete log entry. From Section 6 — every symptom has a document path. These six principles cover the full operational and service workflow for RailKing Gen6 equipment.

screens.label: Best Practices
screens.vo: These best practices represent the habits that separate reliable operators from reactive ones. Log every shift. Reduce load before the alarm. Allow proper warm-up and cool-down. Inspect after every operation. Accept the machine only after you have checked it yourself. The always and never grid on the right side of this screen is the short version — the list you carry in your head every day when you get in the cab.

exam.instr_title:
RailKing Gen6 Operations and Service Fundamentals
Final Knowledge Assessment
exam.pass_msg: You have achieved a passing score and earned completion credit for KIR-RK-G6-001.
exam.fail_msg: A score of 80% is required to pass. Review the recommended sections and schedule a retake.
section_names: Section 1 - Documentation, Safety, and Support Workflow
Section 4 - Normal Operation and Performance Monitoring
Section 5 - Shutdown, Recovery, and Daily Service Interface

questions.stem: Which file group is primary for support contact routing?
questions.feedback: Support contact routing should begin with contact and support form references.

questions.stem: Warranty context should be considered before non-routine actions.
questions.feedback: True. Warranty and responsibility boundaries influence escalation and service actions.

questions.stem: Which manuals represent model-level guidance?
questions.feedback: Model-level guidance is in the RK22, RK23, and RK24 manuals.

questions.stem: You can safely apply a procedure across models without checking model documentation.
questions.feedback: False. Always confirm model-specific procedures before application.

questions.stem: What precedes startup command execution?
questions.feedback: Pre-operation readiness checks come before startup execution.

questions.stem: Hold points are used when startup criteria are uncertain.
questions.feedback: True. Hold points prevent progression under uncertain conditions.

questions.stem: How should warning indicators be handled during normal operation?
questions.feedback: Warnings should be handled through procedure-based response logic.

questions.stem: Operator handoff quality affects troubleshooting efficiency.
questions.feedback: True. Incomplete handoffs increase rework and delay diagnosis.

questions.stem: What is the objective of controlled shutdown?
questions.feedback: Controlled shutdown ensures safe transition to post-operation state.

questions.stem: Post-operation inspection can be skipped if no alarms occurred.
questions.feedback: False. Post-operation inspection is required regardless of alarm presence.

questions.stem: What is the first advanced troubleshooting action in RK24 references?
questions.feedback: Start by selecting the correct technical reference domain.

questions.stem: C5 is the drawing set and C6 is the OEM vendor document set.
questions.feedback: True. Both sets are used together for advanced RK24 support.

questions.stem: Which practice improves first-pass support quality?
questions.feedback: Complete structured support data improves first-pass quality.

questions.stem: Model-specific manuals are optional when thresholds appear familiar.
questions.feedback: False. Familiarity does not replace model-specific validation.

questions.stem: What supports startup continuity between shifts?
questions.feedback: Startup records support continuity and traceability.

questions.stem: Warning response should consider escalation criteria.
questions.feedback: True. Escalation criteria are part of warning response workflow.

questions.stem: A complete service handoff packet includes:
options: forms, findings, references consulted, pending actions
questions.feedback: Comprehensive handoff content reduces support iteration cycles.

questions.stem: OEM references should be used without service-manual context.
questions.feedback: False. Use OEM details with service-manual system context.

questions.stem: Which section emphasizes model family comparison?
questions.feedback: Section 2 addresses platform overview and model comparisons.

questions.stem: Which section emphasizes RK24 drawing and OEM document navigation?
questions.feedback: Section 6 is dedicated to advanced RK24 reference use.
